reason: The item cleanly targets boundary-aware interpretation of non-monotonic peak overpressure under venting and the meaning of model over-prediction as conservative for pressure-rise rate. Decoys are largely single-fault and engineering-plausible (misreading conservatism, misattributing mechanism, overgeneralizing from rate to peak), yielding solid expert discrimination. Minor risk remains that A vs E feels partially redundant and that option F’s absolute wording makes it slightly easier to eliminate on style rather than analysis, capping neutrality and signal strength.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility: whether established damage thresholds transfer to battery vent-gas deflagrations and what can be inferred from differing gas volumes across chemistries. Decoys are mostly plausible and separable, but a couple invite multi-step interpretive disputes (e.g., impulse vs peak-pressure attribution; “sensitivity to partial-volume effects”), slightly diluting single-fault purity and making expert disagreement more likely on wording nuances rather than core engineering judgment.

The prompt sharply constrains the judgment to whether a 25% well-mixed LFL criterion is adequate given stated full-scale damage at 1–2.5% enclosure-LFL-equivalent release, and it cleanly requires adding one chemistry-dependent hazard factor beyond LFL. It strongly targets boundary-aware reasoning (standard LFL heuristics fail) while keeping the cognitive axis coherent (threshold adequacy + chemistry factor). Main limitation is it is a short-answer item with no explicit decoy options, so discrimination depends on rubric strictness rather than option design, slightly weakening decoy craftsmanship and signal separation.
